title: Hardware-aware Low-latency Quantum Compilation with Data-driven Lightweight Error Detection for Early Fault-Tolerant Systems
abstract: Noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) processors are entering an early fault-tolerance regime where full quantum error correction carries prohibitive resource costs, yet lightweight error detection can meaningfully improve algorithmic success rates. Existing compilation and error-detection toolchains treat these concerns in isolation, with no principled way to balance detection overhead against success probability under latency constraints. We present an integrated hardware-aware compilation and data-driven quantum error-detection (QED) framework that jointly optimises qubit mapping, SWAP insertion, and syndrome-schedule placement via a noise-weighted cost function and a learned multi-objective scheduler. Simulation experiments on an HPC cluster using GPU-accelerated density-matrix simulation (NVIDIA cuQuantum SDK) across VQE, phase-estimation, and Grover benchmarks, three noise profiles, and circuit sizes of 6-20 qubits (depths 10-160), show that joint co-design raises algorithmic success probability by up to 68 percent (95 percent CI: 60 percent to 76 percent) over SABRE on an 8-qubit VQE instance with post-selection.
comment: 16 pages, 15 figures, Springer LNCS format. Code available at https://github.com/Sumitchongder/quantum-hw-aware-pipeline

bite.hook: A new quantum compiler boosts algorithmic success rates by up to 68% using lightweight error detection.
tldr: They developed an integrated, hardware-aware quantum compilation and lightweight error-detection framework.
They co-designed qubit mapping, SWAP insertion, and error-syndrome scheduling using a noise-weighted cost function and a learned scheduler.
They demonstrated up to a 68% increase in success probability over the standard SABRE compiler on an 8-qubit VQE benchmark.
bite.whyItMatters: Building fully error-corrected quantum computers is still years away. This lightweight approach bridges the gap, allowing today's noisy quantum processors to run complex algorithms with much higher reliability without massive hardware overhead.
bite.eli5: Think of a quantum computer like a delivery truck driving on a bumpy road where packages might break. Instead of building an expensive, armored truck, this software smart-maps the route and schedules quick check-ins to catch and discard broken packages, making sure the final delivery is correct.

keyNumbers.label: Maximum success probability increase
keyNumbers.context: The improvement in algorithmic success rate achieved over the SABRE compiler on an 8-qubit VQE instance.

keyNumbers.label: Qubit range
keyNumbers.context: The size of the quantum circuits simulated to benchmark the compilation framework.

glossary.shortDef: Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum; the current era of quantum processors that lack full error correction.
glossary.eli5Def: Today's quantum computers, which have enough qubits to do cool things but are still prone to making mistakes due to environmental noise.

glossary.shortDef: The process of translating high-level quantum algorithms into physical instructions for quantum hardware.
glossary.eli5Def: Translating a high-level computer program into the exact, step-by-step physical nudges a quantum chip needs to execute it.

glossary.shortDef: An error-mitigation technique where experimental runs that trigger error flags are discarded.
glossary.eli5Def: Throwing away the results of a calculation if a warning light goes off during the run, keeping only the clean runs.
